title: STANDARD FOR SMOKED FISH, SMOKE-FLAVOURED FISH AND SMOKE-DRIED FISH

sections.section: STANDARD FOR SMOKED FISH, SMOKE-FLAVOURED FISH AND SMOKE-DRIED FISH
sections.summary: Document title and history: Standard for Smoked Fish, Smoke‑Flavoured Fish and Smoke‑Dried Fish (CXS 311-2013), adopted 2013 and amended in 2016 and 2018.

Scope: applies to smoked, smoke‑flavoured and smoke‑dried fish (whole, fillets, sliced and similar) prepared from fresh, chilled or frozen raw material for direct consumption, further processing, or inclusion in mixed products, and excludes fish treated with carbon monoxide, fish in hermetically sealed commercially sterile containers, and specialty/minced products as such.
Description: introduces separate product and process definitions for smoked fish, smoke‑flavoured fish and smoke‑dried fish addressed in the following subsections.

Smoked fish (section overview): defines smoked fish as fish subjected to hot or cold smoking and provides process definitions (smoking, regenerated smoke, smoke condensates, hot/cold smoking, salting, drying, packaging, storage) used to produce and preserve smoked fish.

Product definition (2.1.1): smoked fish is fish that has undergone a hot or cold smoking process defined in 2.1.2 and whose end product has smoked sensory characteristics; spices and other optional ingredients may be used.

Process definitions (2.1.2): defines 'smoking', 'smoking by regenerated smoke', 'smoke condensates', 'hot smoking' (noting hot smoking is generally sufficient to kill parasites, destroy non‑sporulated bacterial pathogens and injure certain spores), 'cold smoking' (reduces water activity without significant protein coagulation), and related processes such as salting, drying, packaging and storage.

Smoke‑flavoured fish (section overview): defines smoke‑flavoured fish as fish treated with smoke flavours rather than smoked, and provides process definitions for smoke flavours, application methods, smoke condensates, packaging, storage, drying and salting.

Product definition (2.2.1): smoke‑flavoured fish is prepared from fish treated with smoke flavours without undergoing the smoking processes of 2.1 and the end product must have a smoked taste; optional spices/ingredients may be used.

Process definition (2.2.2): defines 'smoke flavours' (smoke condensates or artificial flavour blends), 'smoke flavouring' application methods (dipping, spraying, injecting, soaking), 'smoke condensates', and related packaging, storage, drying and salting processes.

Smoke‑dried fish (section overview): defines smoke‑dried fish as fish subjected to combined smoking and drying (may include salting), and defines 'smoke drying' as producing a product storable/transportable without refrigeration by achieving a water activity ≤0.75 (≈10% moisture) to control bacterial pathogens and fungal spoilage, with related drying, salting, packaging and storage provisions.

Product definition (2.3.1): smoke‑dried fish is prepared by combined smoking and drying (may include salting), using traditional or industrial smoke‑drying, and must have smoke‑dried sensory characteristics; spices and optional ingredients may be used.

'Smoke drying' is defined as combined smoking and drying of fish to render it storable/transportable without refrigeration and achieve water activity ≤ 0.75 (≈≤10% moisture); 'drying', 'salting', 'packaging' and 'storage' are defined as processes to reduce moisture, lower water activity/enhance flavour with food-grade salt, protect product from contamination/rehydration, and keep smoke-dried fish at ambient temperature in conformity with Sections 3 and 6.
Any product presentation is permitted provided it meets all requirements of this Standard and is labelled sufficiently to avoid misleading the consumer.

Sets essential composition and quality: smoked/smoke‑flavoured/smoke‑dried fish must be from sound, wholesome raw fish; ingredients must be food grade; smoke-generating wood/plant material must not contain or be contaminated with toxic substances and must be handled to avoid contamination; susceptible species' product must have ≤10 mg histamine/100 g (sample average) and be free from persistent decomposition odours; final products must meet Sections 8–10/9 testing and compliance provisions.
Smoked, smoke‑flavoured and smoke‑dried fish must be prepared from sound, wholesome fish (fresh, chilled or frozen) of quality suitable for human consumption after appropriate preparation.
Heading only (Ingredients) with no substantive requirements in this section text.

sections.section: 3.3 Wood or other plant material for generation of smoke
sections.summary: Wood or plant material used to generate smoke or smoke condensates must not contain toxic substances (naturally, via contamination, or from chemical treatments such as paint/impregnants) and must be handled to avoid contamination, with reference to the PAH reduction Code of Practice (CXC 68-2009).

For susceptible species, products shall not contain more than 10 mg histamine per 100 g fish flesh (based on the sample unit average) and must be free from persistent or objectionable odours/flavours of decomposition.
Final products must meet this Standard when lots examined per Section 10 comply with provisions in Section 9, and products shall be examined by methods in Section 8.
Heading only (Food additives) with no substantive text in this section.

For smoked fish, acidity regulators, colours and preservatives listed in Tables 1 and 2 of the General Standard for Food Additives (CXS 192-1995) for food category 09.2.5 (and parent categories) and certain Table 3 acidity regulators, antioxidants and packaging gases (as indicated) are acceptable for use.

For smoke‑flavoured fish, the same acidity regulators, colours, preservatives and specified Table 3 substances of CXS 192-1995 applicable to food category 09.2.5 (and parent categories) are acceptable for use.
No additives are permitted in smoke‑dried fish.

Sets contaminant provisions: products must comply with maximum levels in the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Foods and Feed (CXS 193-1995), and smoking should minimize PAH formation following the PAH reduction Code of Practice (CXC 68-2009).
Products covered by this Standard shall comply with the maximum contaminant and toxin levels established in the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Foods and Feed (CXS 193-1995).

Smoking should be performed to minimise formation of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAH), for example by following the Code of Practice for Reduction of PAH from Smoking and Direct Drying (CXC 68-2009).

Products must be prepared and handled per the General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1-1969) and relevant Codex codes; they must meet established microbiological criteria, contain no living parasites (cold‑smoked or smoke‑flavoured products should be frozen if parasite hazards exist and parasite viability is to be examined per Sections 8.10/8.11), comply with Listeria monocytogenes guidance (CXG 61-2007), contain no Clostridium botulinum toxins and control botulinum risk via measures such as packaging, storage temperature and water activity, and must not exceed 20 mg histamine/100 g fish flesh in any sample unit for susceptible species while being free from other hazardous substances. In addition, labelling must follow the General Standard for the Labelling of Prepackaged Foods (CXS 1-1985) with specific naming ('smoked X', 'smoke flavoured X', 'smoke‑dried X'), possible indication of regenerated smoke where required by the country, required storage/handling instructions, and specified information for non‑retail containers.

Products covered by this Standard must be prepared and handled in accordance with the General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1-1969) and other relevant Codex texts such as the Code of Practice for Fish and Fishery Products (CXC 52-2003).
Heading only (Microbiological criteria) with substantive criteria referenced elsewhere (e.g., Principles and Guidelines CXG 21-1997).

Products shall not contain living parasites; cold‑smoked or smoke‑flavoured products should be frozen before or after smoking if a parasite hazard exists, and viability of nematodes, cestodes and trematodes shall be examined according to Sections 8.10 and/or 8.11.

Ready‑to‑eat products must comply with the microbiological criteria for Listeria monocytogenes elaborated in Annex II of the Guidelines on the Application of General Principles of Food Hygiene to the Control of Listeria Monocytogenes in Foods (CXG 61-2007).

Toxins of Clostridium botulinum are prohibited in smoked, smoke‑flavoured and smoke‑dried fish products, and toxin formation must be controlled using science‑based combinations (e.g., packaging type, storage temperature, water activity or salt in the water phase) as illustrated in Annex 2; consuming countries may permit uneviscerated product or require evisceration before or after processing to minimize C. botulinum risk.

Product shall not contain histamine exceeding 20 mg/100 g fish flesh in any sample unit, applied only to susceptible species (e.g. Scombridae, Clupeidae, Engraulidae, Coryphaenidae, Pomatomidae, Scomberesocidae).
Products must not contain any other substance at levels that present a health hazard according to Codex standards, and final product must be free from foreign material that threatens human health.
Heading introducing specific labelling provisions that apply in addition to the General Standard for the Labelling of Prepackaged Foods (CXS 1‑1985).
The food name must indicate the process—'smoked X', 'smoke flavoured X' or 'smoke‑dried X'—with X the common/usual species name as required by local law or custom so as not to mislead the consumer.
Countries where the product is sold may require the label to indicate the use of regenerated smoke.
Labels must declare storage and handling instructions appropriate for the product.

Information required for retail labelling may be provided on non‑retail containers or accompanying documents, but the product name, lot identification, manufacturer/packer name and address, and storage instructions must appear on the container.
Section heading introducing sampling, examination and analysis requirements for the standard.
Section heading for sampling provisions.
Sensory and physical examinations shall follow an attribute sampling plan per General Guidelines on Sampling (CXG 50‑2004, Section 4.2, Table 10) using AQL 6.5%.
Net weight determination sampling shall use the s‑method (sampling by variables with unknown standard deviation) per CXG 50‑2004, Section 4.3, Table 14.

Samples for sensory and physical examination must be assessed by trained personnel following procedures in Sections 8.4–8.6 and the Guidelines for the Sensory Evaluation of Fish and Shellfish in Laboratories (CXG 31‑1999).

Defines method performance criteria for histamine determination, including MLs of 10 mg/100 g (average) and 20 mg/100 g (per unit) with specified applicable ranges, LOD/LOQ, RSDR, recoveries and acceptable methods (e.g., AOAC 977.13, NMKL 99, NMKL 196).
Net weight is defined as the weight of the product exclusive of packaging, interleaving material and similar non‑product items.
Frozen final product samples shall be thawed at refrigeration temperatures to maintain quality and safety.

Microbiological criteria for Listeria monocytogenes use ISO 11290‑2 for products where growth will not occur and ISO 11290‑1 where growth can occur, with other methods accepted if they provide equivalent validated sensitivity, reproducibility and reliability (e.g., per ISO 16140).
Detection of C. botulinum and its toxins should use AOAC 977.26 or an equivalent validated method; testing is not routine but may be conducted when toxin presence is suspected.

Provides the calculation for percentage salt in the aqueous phase (% salt x 100 / (% water + % salt)) and references AOAC methods for determining moisture (AOAC 952.08) and salt (AOAC 937.09) in seafood.
Water activity must be determined using NMKL 168 or ISO 21807.

sections.section: 8.10 Determination of the viability of parasites
sections.summary: Methods for extracting and testing parasite viability may follow the nematode method in Annex I of the Standard for Salted Atlantic Herring and Salted Sprat (CXS 244-2004) or other validated methods acceptable to the competent authority having jurisdiction.

Visible-parasite inspection requires non-destructive examination of the entire (thawed if necessary) sample unit by placing portions on a 5 mm thick acrylic sheet (45% translucency) and candling with a 1500 lux light source positioned 30 cm above the sheet.
Defines a defective sample unit as one exhibiting any of the properties specified in the following subsections (foreign matter, parasites, odour/flavour/texture) as set out in Section 9.

Foreign-matter defect is defined as any matter not derived from the fish that does not threaten human health and is readily recognized without magnification or is present at a level (including as detected by magnification) indicating non-compliance with good manufacturing practice.

A parasite defect is the presence of two or more visible parasites per kg of sample unit (detected by the method in 8.11) with a capsular diameter >3 mm, or any non-encapsulated parasite >10 mm in length.

A sensory defect is a sample unit exhibiting persistent, distinct objectionable odours, flavours, or textures indicative of decomposition, rancidity, burning sensation, or other impressions not characteristic of the product.

A lot meets the standard when (i) the total number of defectives per Section 9 does not exceed the acceptance number (c) of an AQL‑6.5 sampling plan, (ii) the average net weight is not less than the declared weight with no individual container under 95% of declared and no unreasonable shortage, and (iii) essential composition, quality, additives, contaminants, hygiene, handling and labelling requirements of Sections 3–7 are met. For histamine, no sample unit shall exceed 20 mg/100 g of fish flesh according to the chosen sampling plan (see Section 8.3).

Methods acceptable to the competent authority must be used to kill parasites, and where freezing is required (e.g., cold-smoked or smoke-flavoured fish) the product must be frozen before or after processing to a temperature–time combination sufficient to inactivate parasites; examples include −20°C at the thermal centre for 24 h (effective for Anisakis spp. and Pseudoterranova decipiens), −35°C for 15 h (all parasites), or −20°C for 168 h (all parasites).

Bibliographic references cited regarding parasite viability and freezing efficacy, including FAO Fisheries Technical Paper 444, Bier (1976), Deardoff et al. (1984), Health and Welfare Canada (1992), and US FDA (2001).
